name: BitDrones
short_description: A toolbox of touchable nano-quadcopters that act as self-levitating, mid-air physical voxels for interactive 3D displays.
long_description: BitDrones is a research prototype that uses swarms of small nano-quadcopters as tangible, self-levitating building blocks for what its authors call a 'real reality' 3D display. Three drone types (PixelDrones with an RGB LED and OLED, ShapeDrones with a geometric mesh body, and DisplayDrones with a thin-film touchscreen) are tracked via motion capture so users can touch, drag, throw, and resize individual drones and compound models in mid-air. It matters as physical AI / interaction design because it explores programmable matter and direct, unmediated tangible manipulation of volumetric information without a headset, prefiguring shape-changing interfaces made of many actuated physical elements.
ai_note: Drones are positioned via external motion-capture tracking and scripted control; no AI/ML runs in the system.

images.caption: A user touching a ShapeDrone among a group of self-levitating BitDrones acting as mid-air physical voxels.
images.alt_text: Small quadcopter drones hovering in mid-air, each fitted with a geometric body, while a person's hand reaches in to touch one of them.

sources.title: BitDrones: Towards Using 3D Nanocopter Displays as Interactive Self-Levitating Programmable Matter
BitDrones: Interactive Flying Microbots Show Future of Virtual Reality is Physical
Queen's University professor to unveil self-levitating displays, allowing physical interactions with mid-air virtual objects

interactions.title: Grab a pixel out of the air
interactions.description: Instead of looking at voxels on a screen, you reach into a cluster of hovering PixelDrones and physically touch, drag, or nudge one — each drone is a self-levitating pixel you handle with your bare hand, no headset required.

interactions.title: Resize a model by stretching it with two hands
interactions.description: The system supports unimanual and bimanual techniques, so you can two-hand a compound model and pull it wider or pinch it smaller, the constituent drones flying apart or together to follow your gesture.

interactions.title: Throw a drone across the room
interactions.description: Beyond touch and drag, you can throw an individual drone to send it to a new location in the volumetric workspace, treating mid-air data objects with the same physicality as real objects.

interactions.title: Open a folder and watch it bloom into a cone tree
interactions.description: Touch a PixelDrone representing a folder and its contents fan out as other drones hovering in a horizontal wheel beneath it; you browse the files by physically swiping the drones left or right.

interactions.title: Assemble a 3D shape from flying blocks
interactions.description: ShapeDrones carry lightweight geometric mesh bodies that act as building blocks, so you snap and stack them in mid-air to compose and edit a real-time three-dimensional model out of actual hovering pieces.

interactions.title: Send a flying face to a meeting you can't attend
interactions.description: A DisplayDrone carrying a thin-film touchscreen and camera becomes a videoconferencing avatar: a remote person joins over Skype, the drone flies around the local space and replicates their head movements, letting them inspect a model and talk to people without a wheeled robot body.

interactions.title: Walk around a building model that floats in the room
interactions.description: An architect could lay out a massing study as a cloud of ShapeDrones at room scale, then walk around it and tug individual volumes higher or wider by hand — editing the design in the same physical space the client is standing in, grounded in the existing touch, drag, and resize techniques.

interactions.title: A self-rearranging desktop notification
interactions.description: Building on the demonstrated self-levitating alert boxes, a single PixelDrone could drift up to eye level and pulse to flag an incoming message, then settle back into the cluster once you swat it away — a notification you dismiss with a wave instead of a click.
